title: Building luxury homes is good for the poor - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Citing Tej Parikh's FT piece, Alex Tabarrok reviews international 'filtering' evidence from Honolulu, Helsinki, Sweden, San Francisco, and New York showing new market-rate and even luxury housing construction lowers costs and eviction risk for lower-income residents, undercutting arguments that luxury development harms affordability.

themes.theme: Filtering: how luxury supply lowers prices down the chain

insights.claim: High-end housing developments unlock long housing chains: as higher-income households move into newly built units, they free up older properties, raising supply and lowering prices for middle- and lower-end housing, a process known as filtering.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Establishes the core economic mechanism supporting pro-supply, deregulatory housing policy as an affordability tool.
insights.locator: high-end developments unlock long housing chains

insights.claim: A study tracking households that moved into a newly built 512-unit condominium tower in Honolulu found the building created at least 557 vacancies in older, cheaper apartments across the city within three years, with market-rate units generating the largest chains.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Quantifies the multiplier effect of a single luxury development on the broader affordable-housing stock -- a concrete data point for modeling filtering impact from new supply.
insights.locator: One published last year tracked households

insights.claim: A 2021 Helsinki study using geo-coded population data found that every 100 new market-rate units built in the city centre led to about 60 units becoming available in the city's bottom half of neighborhoods by income.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Provides a specific conversion ratio (100 new central units -> ~60 units freed for lower-income areas) usable for estimating filtering effects elsewhere.

insights.claim: A multi-decade analysis across all homes in Sweden concluded that new homes, even those initially inhabited primarily by rich people, lead to substantial trickle-down effects that also benefit the poor.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Long-run, national-scale evidence strengthens the case that supply-side housing policy benefits low-income residents even when new construction targets the wealthy.
insights.quote: new homes, even those initially primarily inhabited by rich people, lead to substantial trickle-down effects that also benefit the poor
insights.locator: An analysis across all homes in Sweden

insights.claim: A 2021 San Francisco paper found new market-rate developments lowered the risk of eviction notices for nearby residents in rent-stabilized housing.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Shows a direct tenant-protection benefit from new supply, relevant to renter-protection policy debates that often oppose new luxury construction.
insights.locator: In San Francisco, a 2021 paper found

insights.claim: Even 'luxury' developments in New York City -- the type criticized by Mamdani -- have been shown to contribute to lower local rents and sales prices.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Directly rebuts a live political argument (attributed to Mamdani) against luxury development, relevant to NYC housing/zoning policy and real estate investment decisions.
insights.locator: Even 'luxury' developments in New York City

insights.claim: The cumulative international filtering evidence from Honolulu, Helsinki, Sweden, San Francisco, and New York undercuts the common political argument that building luxury housing hurts affordability for the poor.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Supports a pro-supply investment and policy thesis for real estate and urban development, against rent-control/anti-luxury-development narratives.
insights.locator: Numerous international studies underscore this positive ripple effect
